title: Places of Interest
overview: The United Kingdom is rich in historic landmarks and cultural sites that reflect centuries of change. From prehistoric monuments like Stonehenge to royal palaces and national parks, these places attract millions of visitors every year. They remind people of the nation's shared story and remain central to both British identity and tourism today.

study_sections.heading: Historic Sites
study_sections.content: Stonehenge (Wiltshire) dates from around 3000 BC. Hadrian's Wall marks the northern frontier of Roman Britain. The Tower of London was built by William the Conqueror in the 11th century. Windsor Castle is the oldest occupied royal castle in the world. Palace of Westminster houses UK Parliament.

study_sections.heading: Natural Landscapes
study_sections.content: Lake District National Park inspired poet William Wordsworth. Ben Nevis is the UK's highest mountain in Scotland. Giant's Causeway in Northern Ireland is formed from volcanic rock. Snowdonia (Eryri) attracts mountain walkers.

study_sections.heading: Cultural Sites
study_sections.content: Edinburgh Castle dominates Scotland's capital. Stratford-upon-Avon is Shakespeare's birthplace. Bath is known for Roman baths and Georgian architecture. Cambridge and Oxford are world-famous university cities. Caernarfon Castle is a Welsh UNESCO World Heritage Site.

study_sections.heading: Museums and Attractions
study_sections.content: British Museum houses the Rosetta Stone. Tate Modern displays modern art. National Gallery shows Turner and Constable paintings. Belfast Titanic Museum tells the ship's story. National Trust protects heritage buildings across the UK. The UK has 32 UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

key_facts: The UK is rich in historic, cultural, and natural sites that tell its national story.
In London, landmarks include the Houses of Parliament and Big Ben, symbols of democracy.
Buckingham Palace serves as the monarch’s official residence.
The Tower of London is a World Heritage Site that has been a royal fortress and prison.
St Paul’s Cathedral, designed by Sir Christopher Wren, reflects the city’s resilience after the Great Fire of 1666.
The British Museum holds artefacts from global civilisations such as the Rosetta Stone and Elgin Marbles.
Edinburgh Castle and the Royal Mile showcase Scottish heritage.
Cardiff Castle and the Millennium Centre combine Welsh history with modern culture.
The Giant’s Causeway in Northern Ireland features basalt columns and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Stonehenge and Hadrian’s Wall highlight prehistoric and Roman history.
Canterbury Cathedral and York Minster are major centres of Christian worship and architecture.
Bath is known for its Roman baths and Georgian architecture, while Stratford-upon-Avon marks Shakespeare’s birthplace.
Oxford and Cambridge are famed for historic universities and scholarship.
National parks such as the Lake District, Peak District, Snowdonia, Cairngorms, and Mourne Mountains protect natural landscapes.
Organisations like the National Trust and Historic England preserve historic buildings, gardens, and countryside.
Tourism brings millions of visitors each year, reflecting national pride in a blend of ancient and modern landmarks.
memory_hook: Imagine exploring a living history book—each UK landmark, from Parliament to Stonehenge, opens a new chapter in the nation’s story.

supportive_messages.low_score: Places take time to learn. Keep studying! 💪
